title: Which Glasses Suit My Face? A Shape-by-Shape Buying Guide
description: Find the best glasses for your face shape — round, square, oval, heart, or diamond. Practical tips on frame shapes, sizes, and styles with visual comparisons.
tldr: The core principle: choose frames that contrast your face shape. Round faces suit angular frames, square faces suit curved frames, oval faces suit almost everything. But fit and proportion matter just as much as shape — a well-fitted "wrong" shape often looks better than a poorly-fitted "right" one.

definition.text: Choosing glasses that suit your face involves matching frame shapes to your facial proportions — generally using contrast (angular frames for round faces, curved frames for angular faces) while ensuring proper fit through measurements of frame width, bridge, and temple length.

sections.h2: How to Determine Your Face Shape
sections.content:
Stand in front of a mirror (or take a straight-on photo) and look at three things:

  1. Widest point: Is it your forehead, cheekbones, or jaw?
  2. Jawline: Is it angular and defined, or soft and rounded?
  3. Face length vs. width: Is your face roughly as long as it is wide, or noticeably longer?

Round Face

Cheekbones are the widest point. Soft, curved jawline. Face width and length are similar. Think Jack Black or Selena Gomez.

Square Face

Forehead, cheekbones, and jaw are roughly the same width. Strong, angular jawline. Face width and length are similar. Think Angelina Jolie or Brad Pitt.

Oval Face

Slightly longer than wide. Forehead is slightly wider than the jaw. Gentle curves throughout. Think George Clooney or Jessica Alba.

Heart-Shaped Face

Forehead is the widest point. Face narrows to a defined (sometimes pointed) chin. Think Reese Witherspoon or Ryan Gosling.

Diamond Face

Cheekbones are dramatically the widest point. Narrow forehead and narrow jaw. Think Rihanna or Robert Pattinson.

sections.h2: Best Frames for Each Face Shape
sections.content:
Face ShapeBest Frame ShapesAvoidWhy It Works
RoundRectangle, square, browlineRound, small framesAngular lines add definition
SquareRound, oval, cat-eye, aviatorSharp square, geometricCurves soften angular features
OvalAlmost everythingExtremely oversizedBalanced proportions suit all shapes
HeartAviator, bottom-heavy, roundTop-heavy, wide browlineWider bottom balances wider forehead
DiamondCat-eye, oval, browlineNarrow or angularHighlights cheekbones, adds width at brow

These guidelines give you a starting point, not a rulebook. The right frame is ultimately the one that makes you feel confident when you look in the mirror.

The best way to evaluate frame styles is to try them on in person. Photos and online try-on tools give a rough idea, but they can't replicate how a frame feels on your face — the weight distribution, the temple pressure, and how the nose bridge sits. These comfort factors matter more than aesthetics for glasses you'll wear 12+ hours a day.

Charm Optical in Edmonton carries over 500 frames from brands including Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, Prada, and Dolce & Gabbana. Their opticians help you narrow down the options based on your face shape, prescription requirements (especially important for progressives), and personal style preferences. With same-day service for many prescriptions, you can walk in and walk out with new glasses the same day.

sections.h2: Why Fit Matters More Than Shape
sections.content:
A perfectly shaped frame in the wrong size will never look right. Three measurements define fit:

  • Frame width: Should match your face width. The outer edges of the frame should align with the widest point of your face (usually the temples). Hold the frame up and check — if the frame extends well past your face or falls noticeably short, it's the wrong width.
  • Bridge width: The gap between the lenses that sits on your nose. Too narrow pinches and leaves red marks. Too wide slides down constantly. Standard bridges are 16-20mm for adults.
  • Temple length: The arms that reach behind your ears. Too short pulls the frame forward; too long pokes behind your ears. Most adults need 135-145mm temples.

Frame measurements are printed inside the temple arm as three numbers (e.g., 52-18-140). If you have a pair that fits well, use those numbers as a reference when shopping.

At Charm Optical, the opticians measure your face and pull frames across brands that match your dimensions — saving you from trying on dozens of wrong-sized options.

Skin Tone and Frame Colour

Warm skin tones (yellow or peach undertones) pair well with gold, tortoiseshell, warm brown, and olive frame colours. Cool skin tones (pink or blue undertones) suit silver, black, blue, grey, and cool-toned tortoiseshell. Neutral skin tones can wear any colour.

Lifestyle Considerations

Your daily activities matter as much as your face shape:

  • Office professionals: Conservative shapes in metal or thin acetate. Calvin Klein and Polo Ralph Lauren offer clean, professional options.
  • Active lifestyles: Durable, lightweight frames with grippy features. Oakley leads in performance eyewear.
  • Fashion-forward: Bold acetate, oversized shapes, and distinctive brands. Gucci, Versace, and Chloe make frames that double as accessories.

Prescription Strength

Strong prescriptions mean thicker lenses. Full-rim frames with smaller lens sizes hide edge thickness best. Rimless frames with high prescriptions can look bulky. High-index lenses also help — ask Charm Optical about lens options.

Face shape guides are helpful for narrowing down options, but nothing replaces trying frames on your actual face. Photos flatten three-dimensional features. Online try-on tools give a rough idea but miss details like how the frame sits on your nose, how the temples feel behind your ears, and how the lens height aligns with your eyes.

This matters even more if you need progressive lenses, where vertical lens alignment directly affects how well the three vision zones work.

The team at Charm Optical helps people match glasses to faces every day. They can assess your face shape, pull frames from Ray-Ban, Oakley, Gucci, Persol, and other brands that suit your proportions, and ensure the fit is right before you commit.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

sections.h2: Insurance Coverage for Glasses in Canada
sections.content:
Most Canadians with employer-sponsored health benefits have optical coverage that helps pay for glasses. Typical plans cover $200-$500 every 1-2 years toward frames and lenses combined. Some plans separate frame and lens allowances, while others combine them into a single optical benefit. The specifics depend on your employer's plan — always check your benefit booklet or call your insurer before shopping.

In Alberta, common insurance providers for optical coverage include Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, Desjardins, and Great-West Life. Government programs like AISH and Alberta Works also provide optical benefits for eligible residents. Children under 18 in Alberta can access basic optical coverage through the Alberta Child Health Benefit.

The easiest way to use your benefits is through direct billing — the optical store submits the claim to your insurer electronically, and you only pay the remaining balance at the counter. Charm Optical in Edmonton offers direct insurance billing for most Alberta insurance providers, including Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins. This means no out-of-pocket costs up front and no paperwork to submit later. book an eye exam online to get your prescription updated and use your benefits before they reset.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter.

Whether you are buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.q: What if I can't tell what face shape I have?
faqs.a: Many people have a combination of features that don't fit neatly into one category — that's normal. Visit Charm Optical and the team will help assess your proportions and recommend frames that complement your specific features, regardless of which "category" your face falls into.

faqs.q: Can I break the face shape rules?
faqs.a: Absolutely. The guidelines exist to help narrow choices, not restrict them. If you try on a pair that "shouldn't" work for your face shape but it looks great, wear it. Personal style and confidence matter more than any rule.

faqs.q: Should I match my glasses to my hairstyle?
faqs.a: Hairstyle affects the overall look but shouldn't override face shape considerations. Glasses sit on your face permanently while hairstyles change. Choose frames based on your face shape and features first, then adjust your hair around them if needed.

faqs.q: Does insurance cover glasses in Canada?
faqs.a: Most employer health plans in Canada include optical coverage of $200-$500 every 1-2 years. Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins are common providers. Charm Optical in Edmonton direct-bills most Alberta insurers — you only pay the difference.

faqs.q: Where is the best place to buy glasses in Edmonton?
faqs.a: Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton carries 500+ designer frames, offers same-day glasses, direct insurance billing, and professional fitting. They carry Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, and more.
